---
title: "Challenge 3. Protecting communications and applications"
subtitle: "Security in Computer Networks"
author: Alejandro Pérez Bueno
bibliography: references.bib
date: today
date-format: "MMM DD, YYYY"
toc: true
toc-title: "Table of Contents"
linkcolor: blue
documentclass: report
link-citations: true
link-bibliography: true
format:
  gfm: default
  pdf:
    linestretch: 1.25
    geometry:
      - top=30mm
      - left=20mm
      - heightrounded
    fontsize: 12pt
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
          \pagestyle{fancy}
          \fancyhead[C]{UOC}
          \fancyhead[R]{aperez-b@uoc.edu}
          \fancyfoot{}
          \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage}
---

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Activity 1

### 1.1

NexusLifePharma is currently sending prescriptions, bank transactions and health data over plain HTTP. This means everything travels in plain text with no server authentication and no integrity check. TLS fixes this by setting up an encrypted channel before any application data gets exchanged [@perramon2023tls].

Below is a diagram of the TLS 1.3 handshake as defined in RFC 8446, which brings the negotiation down to a single round-trip [@rescorla2018].

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client (Browser)
    participant S as Server (NexusLifePharma)

    C->>S: ClientHello<br/>(supported versions, key_share ECDHE, cipher suites)
    S->>C: ServerHello<br/>(chosen version, key_share ECDHE)
    S->>C: {EncryptedExtensions}
    S->>C: {Certificate} (X.509)
    S->>C: {CertificateVerify} (digital signature)
    S->>C: {Finished} (HMAC)
    C->>S: {Finished} (HMAC)
    C->>S: Application Data (AES-256-GCM)
    S->>C: Application Data (AES-256-GCM)
```

::: {.callout-note}

Messages in braces are encrypted.

:::

After the ServerHello everything that follows including the server certificate is encrypted, so a passive observer cannnot even see the server's identity [@rescorla2018; @perramon2023tls].

### 1.2

The table below shows the cryptographic primitives involved in each phase of the TLS handshake [@perramon2023crypto; @perramon2023tls].

| Phase | Cryptographic Primitives | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Authentication | Asymmetric Cryptography, Digital Signature with digital certificates (X.509) | The server sends its X.509 certificate signed by a trusted CA that binds its public key to its identity. The client verifies this signature to make sure it is talking to the real server. |
| Key Exchange | Diffie-Hellman or RSA to generate an ephemeral session key (Forward Secrecy) | Both sides exchange public DH values and each derives the same shared secret without ever transmiting it. Because computing discrete logarithms is hard an eavesdropper cannot recover the secret. |
| Data Ciphering | Symmetric Cryptography (AES-256) | Once the session keys are derived all application data gets encrypted with AES-256. Symmetric encryption is used here because it is much faster than asymmetric for bulk data. |
| Integrity | Hash Functions (HMAC / SHA-2) | Every TLS record includes an HMAC computed over a secret key, an implicit sequence number and the record contents. If anything changes in transit the HMAC will not match and the conection is dropped. |

### 1.3

In older TLS setups that use static RSA key exchange the client encrypts a pre-master secret with the server's long-term RSA public key. So the security of every past session depends on that one private key staying secret [@perramon2023crypto].

If an attacker records encrypted traffic and later gets hold of that private key (through theft, legal pressure or cryptanalysis) they can decrypt every session they previously captured. This is the "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" threat model that we also discussed back in CAA 1 [@perramon2023crypto].

Ephemeral key exchange solves this through Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS). With Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral (DHE) or ECDHE both sides generate fresh key pairs per session and discard them right after. Since the shared secret cannot be reconstructed from the server's long-term key alone, compromissing it later reveals nothing about past sessions [@perramon2023tls; @perramon2023crypto].

TLS 1.3 takes care of this by requiring ephemeral key exchange in every cipher suite. Static RSA key exchange was removed from the specification entirely [@rescorla2018].

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Activity 2

NexusLifePharma needs to pick a session management mechanism for its pharmacist mobile app. JSON Web Tokens (JWT) are stateless tokens defined in RFC 7519 that encode session data into a signed token held by the client [@jones2015]. Server-side sessions on the other hand store the data on the server and only send a session ID to the client through a cookie [@perramon2023tls].

The table below compares both approaches [@jones2015; @owasp2025jwt].

| Criteria | JWT tokens | Sessions (Cookies) |
|---|---|---|
| State storage | Stateless: the token lives on the client side and contains all session data (claims) in a signed JSON object. The server does not keep any state. | Stateful: the server stores session data in memory or a databse and sends the client just a session ID via a cookie. |
| Main risk | Token theft via XSS or man-in-the-middle. Because the token is self-contained a stolen JWT gives full access until it expires. The server cannot invalidate it early without extra infrastructure. | Session hijacking through cookie theft (XSS, network sniffing). But the session can be invalidated immediately on the server by deleting the record. |
| Revocation | Difficult: since JWTs are stateless there is no server record to delete. Revoking a token before expiration means maintaining a blacklist which adds statefulness and kind of defeats the purpose. | Simple: the server deletes or invalidates the session record at any time cutting access for that session ID. |

For a high-traffic environment like this we would go with JWT tokens. Because they are stateless the server does not need to hit a session store on every request, which reduces latency and makes horizontal scaling simpler. Session-based approaches need either sticky sessions or a shared store like Redis which adds complexity [@jones2015]. The main downside of JWTs (hard revocation) can be mitigated with short expiration times combined with refresh tokens.

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Activity 3

Social Security numbers and IBANs have a limited and predictable format. In Spain Social Security numbers are 12-digit numbers and IBANs follow a fixed structure starting with a country code and check digits. Because of this the search space is small enough that an attacker can precompute hashes for every posible value [@perramon2023crypto].

SHA-256 alone is not enough to protect against something like the Energía XXI breach [@energia2026]. SHA-256 is a fast hash function designed for efficency so an attacker with modern hardware can compute billions of hashes per second. On top of that without salting the attacker can build a rainbow table mapping every possible Social Security number or IBAN to its SHA-256 hash. Once the database is exfiltrated reversing the hashes becomes a simple lookup [@perramon2023crypto].

Salting fixes part of the problem by adding random bits to each value before hashing. This way identical inputs produce diferent outputs and precomputation attacks stop working because the attacker has to compute a separate hash for every combination of salt and candidate value [@perramon2023crypto].

To go further we propose combining salting with a Hardware Security Module (HSM). An HSM is a physical device that stores cryptographic keys and does operations inside tamper-resistant hardware so the keys never leave the module [@owasp2025storage]. The architecture would work like this:

1. When a new Social Security number or IBAN is stored the application generates a unique random salt for that record.
2. The application sends the salt and the plaintext value to the HSM which computes an HMAC using a secret key that lives inside the HSM. The result gets stored in the database together with the salt.
3. To verify a value later the application sends the salt and the candidate to the HSM which recomputes the HMAC and returns wether it matches.

Because the HMAC key never leaves the HSM even if an attacker exfiltrates the whole database including salts they cannot compute the HMAC offline. They would need physical access to the HSM itsself [@owasp2025storage].

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Final Reflection

This CAA showed me how cryptographic primitives that I knew separatley actually chain together in TLS to solve authentication, key exchange, ciphering and integrity in one handshake. The Perfect Forward Secrecy part stuck with me the most, the idea that a single static key can retroactively expose years of recorded traffic is something I now think about differently. The Energía XXI case was also a good reminder that hashing is not the same as protection, and the HSM approach with salting is something practical enough that I could bring up at work at Telefónica.

{{< pagebreak >}}

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
